profile.behavior: Actively forages through shrubs, low branches, and ground cover for insects and spiders; frequently holds its tail cocked and delivers a rapid, bubbly song from a perch. Nests in sheltered cavities, including natural holes and nest boxes.
field_marks: Very small, compact, plain gray-brown wren with a flat-headed profile.
Thin, slightly curved bill; upper mandible dark, lower mandible often paler.
Faint pale eyebrow and pale grayish throat; no strong facial pattern.
Fine dark barring across the warmer brown wings and tail.
Short wings and a relatively long, narrow tail, often cocked upward.
profile.habitat: Open woodland, forest edge, scrub, thickets, and brushy clearings with trees or shrubs; also readily uses human-made settings that provide cover and nest cavities. Usually favors structurally varied vegetation rather than dense, closed canopy.

sources.title: Northern House Wren Identification — Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Northern House Wren Life History — Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Northern House Wren Field Guide — Missouri Department of Conservation

findings: Species identity is well supported: the plate shows the compact gray-brown Northern House Wren with a faint eyebrow, pale throat, thin slightly curved bill, fine dark barring on warmer brown wings and tail, short wings, cocked tail, and slender pinkish-gray legs.
Scientific name, family, and all three printed measurements match independent Cornell verification: 11–13 cm length, 10–12 g weight, and 15 cm wingspan.
The habitat, cavity-nesting, low-shrub/ground foraging, insect-and-spider diet, and bubbly song claims align with Cornell and Missouri Department of Conservation guidance.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
The central bird’s anatomy is coherent: one head, one beak, two wings, two legs, and one tail; the separate wing and head studies are clearly labeled anatomical details rather than additional birds.
